no_data_warning: No data available for the selected filters. Please adjust your filters.
try_different: Try selecting different dates, city, or hotel.
showing_records: Showing {count} records for the selected filters.
no_trend_data: No trend data available. Ensure date range contains valid data.
built_with: Hotel Price Analytics Dashboard | Built with Streamlit
checkout_after_checkin: Check-out date must be after check-in date.
no_season_data_for_range: No data is available for {season} within the selected date range.
try_different_season_or_dates: Try selecting a different season or adjusting the date range.
date_range_adjusted_to_season: Date range adjusted to match the selected season.
season_date_mismatch_explanation: Your selected dates were outside the {season} season. The date range was adjusted to show available data for this season.
econometric_regression_description: Here we take the raw hotel prices and let a regression tell us how much of the level comes from season, city, the specific hotel and the passage of time. Each coefficient is in USD and shows how much the price changes when that factor moves by one unit, while the others are kept fixed; robust (HC1) standard errors are used so that the uncertainty around these numbers is measured reliably.
econometric_anova_description: Here we look only at seasons and ask a very simple question: are the average prices in different seasons really different, or could the gaps be due to random noise? The one-way ANOVA test answers this using an F‑statistic and a p‑value.
econ_insight_season_increase: {season} increases prices by approximately {percent:+.1f}% relative to {baseline_season}, holding city and hotel constant.
econ_insight_season_reduce: {season} reduces prices by approximately {percent:+.1f}% relative to {baseline_season}, holding city and hotel constant.
econ_insight_city: City effect: prices in {city} are about {percent:.1f}% {direction} than in {baseline_city}, after controlling for season and hotel.
econ_insight_trend: Time trend: prices move by roughly {annual_change:+.2f} USD per year ({annual_pct:+.1f}% of the average level), indicating a {direction} drift over time.
econ_insight_significance: {sig_count} out of {total_count} explanatory coefficients are statistically significant at the 5% level (robust standard errors).
econ_insight_anova_sig: Seasonal effects are statistically significant (p < 0.05): average prices differ meaningfully across seasons.
econ_insight_anova_nonsig: Seasonal effects are not statistically significant at the 5% level: average prices across seasons are relatively similar.
econ_insight_season_pattern: Seasonal pattern: {max_season} is the most expensive season and {min_season} is the cheapest; on average, {max_season} prices are about {diff_pct:.1f}% higher than {min_season}.
yoy_requires_multiple_years: Year-over-year analysis requires data from multiple years.
forecasts_placeholder: Forecasting models will be available in the next iteration.
login_error_missing_secret: Application password is not configured. Please contact the administrator.
missing_file: Data file not found. Please check the server configuration.
about_model_intro: This section gives a clear, non-technical explanation of how the econometric model works and how to read its numbers. The same ideas are used in academic work, but here the goal is practical: to understand what drives hotel prices in Armenia and by roughly how much.
about_model_type: The core model is a Multiple Linear Regression estimated using Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) with heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors (HC1).
about_model_formula: price_per_night = α + seasonal_effects + city_effects + hotel_fixed_effects + time_trend + error_term
about_model_formula_explanation: The model decomposes price per night into a baseline intercept (α), seasonal deviations from winter, city-specific effects relative to Yerevan, hotel-specific fixed effects, a linear time trend, and an error term capturing unobserved factors.
about_model_dependent_explanation: The dependent variable is price_per_night, measured in USD. The model uses price levels rather than logarithmic transformations, providing direct interpretability of coefficients in dollar terms. This specification is appropriate for understanding absolute price differences across seasons, cities, and hotels.
about_model_independent_intro: The model includes four categories of explanatory variables, each capturing distinct dimensions of price variation:
about_model_season_baseline: Winter serves as the reference category (baseline). The model estimates coefficients for Spring, Summer, and Autumn relative to Winter.
about_model_season_interpretation: Seasonal coefficients indicate the dollar amount by which prices in a given season differ from winter prices, holding all other factors constant. For example, a positive Summer coefficient of +X USD means summer prices are X dollars higher than winter prices for the same hotel in the same city.
about_model_season_reference: Winter is chosen as the baseline because it represents the most stable pricing period with the least tourism-driven volatility.
about_model_city_baseline: Yerevan serves as the reference category. The model estimates coefficients for Gyumri relative to Yerevan.
about_model_city_interpretation: City coefficients capture systematic price differences between cities, controlling for season, hotel quality, and time trends. These effects reflect differences in market structure, demand patterns, and competitive dynamics between Armenia's major urban centers.
about_model_city_importance: City effects are economically meaningful in Armenia due to distinct tourism markets, infrastructure differences, and varying levels of international visitor concentration between the capital and regional centers.
about_model_hotel_explanation: The model includes fixed effects for each hotel using the C(hotel_name) specification. One hotel is automatically omitted as the reference category.
about_model_hotel_controls: Hotel fixed effects control for time-invariant hotel characteristics that influence pricing, including quality, reputation, location-specific advantages, brand value, and other unobserved hotel-specific factors that remain constant over the analysis period.
about_model_hotel_reference: The omitted hotel serves as the baseline against which all other hotels' price premiums or discounts are measured.
about_model_trend_explanation: The time_trend variable represents the number of days elapsed since the first observation in the dataset. It captures systematic price movements over time that are not explained by seasonal patterns.
about_model_trend_purpose: Including a time trend accounts for long-term market dynamics such as inflation, market maturation, changes in overall demand, and gradual shifts in pricing strategies.
about_model_trend_interpretation: The coefficient on time_trend indicates the daily change in price. To interpret yearly changes, multiply the coefficient by 365. A positive coefficient suggests upward price pressure over time, while a negative coefficient indicates downward pressure.
about_model_winter_reliability: Why Winter Results Are Most Reliable
about_model_winter_stable: The model provides the most statistically stable and interpretable results during the winter season. This reliability stems from several structural characteristics of winter pricing:
about_model_winter_stable_demand: Winter exhibits more stable demand patterns with fewer extreme price shocks. The absence of major tourism events and festivals reduces volatility.
about_model_winter_less_volatility: There is less tourism-driven volatility during winter months. Prices reflect more fundamental market conditions rather than temporary demand spikes.
about_model_winter_predictable: Coefficients estimated using winter data are more stable and provide better predictive power. The relationships between explanatory variables and prices are less contaminated by seasonal noise.
about_model_summer_challenges: In contrast, summer prices are affected by multiple confounding factors:
about_model_summer_tourism: Tourism spikes create temporary demand surges that may not reflect underlying market fundamentals.
about_model_summer_events: Special events, festivals, and conferences introduce exogenous price shocks that complicate interpretation.
about_model_summer_capacity: Capacity constraints during peak periods create non-linear pricing dynamics that are less well-captured by linear models.
about_model_winter_conclusion: Therefore, while the model provides valid estimates across all seasons, coefficients and predictions derived from winter data offer the highest degree of statistical confidence and economic interpretability.
about_model_significance_explanation: Each coefficient is tested for statistical significance using robust standard errors. The p-value is the number that shows how compatible the estimated effect is with the hypothesis “there is no effect at all”: if it is small, the data are hard to reconcile with zero; if it is large, the data are also consistent with having no effect.
about_model_significance_threshold: The usual rule of thumb is p < 0.05. This means: if the true effect were zero, we would see a coefficient at least this large in absolute value less than 5% of the time. If the p-value is below this level, we treat the effect as statistically different from zero; if it is higher, we treat it as statistically uncertain.
about_model_significance_meaningful: R-squared is the number that shows which share of price variation the model can explain. If R-squared is closer to 1, the model follows the data more closely; if it is closer to 0, the model leaves most of the variation unexplained. A high R-squared does not guarantee a good model, but a very low one usually means that important drivers of prices are missing.
about_model_significance_nonmeaningful: Statistical and economic significance are not the same. A coefficient can be statistically significant but economically tiny (for example, +0.01 USD per night, which nobody cares about in practice), or economically large but statistically imprecise because the data are noisy. When reading the table, we always ask two questions: is the number reliably different from zero, and if it moves up or down, would that matter for real decisions?
about_model_anova_explanation: The ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) test is a compact way to compare average prices across several groups at once. Here the groups are seasons, and the question is: are the observed gaps between seasonal averages too large to be explained by random noise alone?
about_model_anova_fstat: The F-statistic is the number that summarizes how big the differences between seasonal means are relative to the variation within each season. If this number is large, it means the between-season gaps dominate; if it is small, it means most variation happens inside seasons, not between them.
about_model_anova_pvalue: The p-value from the ANOVA test is read the same way as for regression coefficients: it shows how likely it is to see seasonal gaps of this size if, in truth, all seasons had the same mean price. If the p-value is below 0.05, we usually say that seasonal differences in average prices are statistically meaningful; if it is higher, the data do not give a clear signal.
about_model_anova_complement: ANOVA complements regression by stripping the problem down to a single dimension (season) and checking whether there is something there even before controlling for city, hotel and time. When both ANOVA and regression point in the same direction, we can be more confident that seasonality is a real and robust pattern.
about_model_practical_hotel: For hotel owners and managers, the coefficients are simply structured ways of comparing prices that already appear on the market:
about_model_practical_hotel_season: Seasonal coefficients show by how many USD the typical price in a given season moves away from the winter level, holding city, hotel and time fixed. If the coefficient for a season is higher, that season tends to be more expensive; if it is lower or negative, that season tends to be cheaper than winter.
about_model_practical_hotel_city: City coefficients show the systematic price gap between cities after aligning for season, hotel and time. If the coefficient for Gyumri is positive, then—other things equal—rooms in Gyumri are priced above comparable rooms in Yerevan; if it is negative, Gyumri is cheaper than Yerevan in comparable conditions.
about_model_practical_hotel_trend: The time-trend coefficient shows how prices drift over the calendar. If this number is clearly above zero, prices tend to creep up over time; if it is clearly below zero, prices tend to become cheaper; if it is close to zero, there is no strong upward or downward trend in the data.
about_model_practical_analyst: For data analysts and researchers, the same coefficients can be read as:
about_model_practical_analyst_rigor: A transparent regression set-up that keeps the structure close to standard OLS, so that assumptions and diagnostics are easy to discuss.
about_model_practical_analyst_quantifica: A way to turn broad statements like “summer is more expensive” or “Yerevan is pricier than Gyumri” into explicit numbers in USD, which can then be compared across factors and over time.
about_model_practical_analyst_validation: A basic but solid check that the main patterns visible in charts (seasonality, city gaps, hotel differences) also show up when we control for everything at once using regression.
about_model_practical_serious: The same econometric tools that are used in research and consulting are applied here, but the goal is straightforward reading of numbers rather than formal theory. Robust standard errors make the inference less sensitive to unequal noise across hotels and dates, so that conclusions about which effects matter are more trustworthy in practice.
econ_academic_title: How to Read the Econometric Results
econ_academic_intro: On this page we walk through the main numbers in the regression tables and explain them in plain language. First we discuss what the coefficients say about prices and hotel differences, then we talk about how the model is built, and only at the end we touch on statistics like R-squared, p-values and ANOVA.
econ_section_purpose_title: What the Numbers Say About Prices
econ_section_purpose_why_model: The first thing the regression gives us is a baseline price level: the intercept. This number shows the typical price per night for a stay in the reference hotel in Yerevan in winter at the start of the sample; if it is higher, it means the whole market is operating at a generally more expensive level, and if it is lower, the baseline market is cheaper.
econ_section_purpose_question: Seasonal coefficients then show, in dollars, how far each season moves away from that winter baseline. Each of these numbers answers a simple question: if we keep the same hotel, the same city and the same point in time, by how many USD does the price change when we switch from winter to this season? If the coefficient for a season is positive and large, that season systematically brings higher prices; if it is negative, that season is systematically cheaper than winter.
econ_section_purpose_ceteris: Hotel fixed-effect coefficients tell us how different hotels are from the reference hotel once we hold season, city and time fixed. Each coefficient here shows the average price premium or discount in USD for that hotel in otherwise identical conditions: if the number is higher, the hotel typically charges more than the reference hotel; if it is lower, the hotel typically charges less. This is why we can say that we compare hotels “fairly”: the model keeps season, city and calendar date unchanged and lets only the hotel vary.
econ_section_specification_dependent: Dependent Variable: The model explains price_per_night in USD. This is the single outcome we track and interpret everywhere in the tables.
econ_section_specification_independent: Independent Variables: To explain price_per_night, the model uses four blocks of explanatory variables:
econ_section_specification_season: Season dummies: Spring, Summer and Autumn relative to Winter (baseline). These capture systematic seasonal shifts in demand and pricing.
econ_section_specification_city: City dummies: Gyumri relative to Yerevan (baseline). These capture the location effect coming from different tourism intensity, income of visitors and local market conditions.
econ_section_specification_hotel: Hotel fixed effects: One coefficient per hotel (with one omitted reference hotel). These absorb all time‑invariant differences in quality, reputation, brand and location that are specific to each hotel.
econ_section_specification_trend: Time trend: A linear trend counting days since the first observation. This picks up gradual movements in the overall level of prices which are not tied to any one season or hotel.
econ_section_specification_levels: Why price levels instead of logs? All coefficients are in USD, so they can be read directly: “this effect adds X dollars to the nightly rate” or “this effect reduces the price by Y dollars”. For business and policy discussions, this is often more intuitive than percentage changes, and it keeps the link to observed prices very direct.
econ_section_coefficients_title: Interpreting Key Coefficients One by One
econ_section_coefficients_intercept_expl: The intercept (α) is the model’s starting point: it is the expected price per night for the reference hotel in Yerevan in Winter on the first day in the sample. This number shows the baseline level of the market; if it is higher, all other prices tend to be built on top of a more expensive base, and if it is lower, the whole price structure starts from a cheaper base.
econ_section_coefficients_seasonal_expla: Each seasonal coefficient (β_season) measures the dollar difference in price between that season and Winter, holding city, hotel and time constant. This number shows how much more (or less) expensive the same room becomes purely because of the season: if the coefficient is positive and large, the season adds a noticeable mark‑up to the winter price; if it is close to zero, prices in that season are similar to winter; if it is negative, that season is cheaper than winter for the same hotel and city.
econ_section_coefficients_seasonal_basel: Winter is used as the reference season because winter demand is more stable and less driven by short‑term tourism shocks. In practice this means that all other seasonal coefficients can be read as clean uplifts or discounts relative to a calm, well‑behaved period of the year.
econ_section_coefficients_city_explanati: The city coefficient captures the systematic price difference between Yerevan and Gyumri once we have aligned for season, hotel and time. This number shows how much the typical price changes when we move the same hotel from Yerevan to Gyumri in the model: if it is positive, Gyumri comes out more expensive in comparable situations; if it is negative, Gyumri is cheaper than Yerevan after controlling for other factors.
econ_section_coefficients_city_interpret: Economically this city gap reflects differences in demand density, purchasing power of visitors, the concentration of tourism activity and local competition in the two cities. A larger absolute value means that location itself is a strong driver of prices; a value close to zero means that, after we control for hotel quality and season, the two cities do not differ much in their typical price level.
econ_section_coefficients_hotel_why: We include hotel fixed effects to let each hotel have its own average price level, independent of season, city and time. These coefficients soak up all stable hotel characteristics that matter for pricing but are not explicitly in the dataset, such as perceived quality, brand strength, repeat‑guest loyalty or a “good address” in the city.
econ_section_coefficients_hotel_quality: Quality: star category, room comfort, service level and similar features that rarely change over the sample period.
econ_section_coefficients_hotel_reputati: Reputation: long‑run customer ratings, word‑of‑mouth, brand name and the trust it carries.
econ_section_coefficients_hotel_location: Location: central vs peripheral areas, distance to key sights, business districts or transport hubs.
econ_section_coefficients_hotel_amenitie: Amenities: presence and quality of facilities such as pool, spa, restaurant or conference rooms.
econ_section_coefficients_hotel_comparis: Without fixed effects, raw price differences between hotels would be a mix of true pricing decisions and differences in underlying quality. Fixed effects separate these: each coefficient shows the average mark‑up or discount of a hotel relative to the reference hotel once we have aligned for season, city and time.
econ_section_coefficients_hotel_interpre: In practice, a positive fixed‑effect coefficient means that the hotel usually charges more per night than the reference hotel in the same season, city and calendar date; a negative coefficient means it usually charges less. This is why we can say that the comparison between hotels is controlled and fair: the model holds season, city and time constant and lets only the "hotel effect" speak.
econ_section_comparison_intro: The fixed‑effect coefficients make it possible to talk about concrete hotels instead of abstract averages. Below we look at a few examples and read them as simple price premiums or discounts relative to the reference hotel, with season, city and time held fixed.
econ_section_comparison_example: Hotel {hotel} has a fixed‑effect coefficient of {coef:.2f} USD. This number shows how much more or less this hotel usually charges per night than the reference hotel in the same season, in the same city and on the same calendar date: if the coefficient is higher, {hotel} is systematically more expensive; if it is lower, {hotel} is systematically cheaper in comparable situations.
econ_section_comparison_economic: Economically, these differences summarize the value of the hotel's brand, location and quality as the market sees them. A stable positive premium means that guests are willing to pay extra for that hotel’s characteristics; a stable discount means the hotel has to undercut the reference price to attract demand.
econ_section_comparison_controlled: We stress that these are controlled comparisons: the model keeps season, city and time unchanged and only switches the hotel. This is why we can say that we compare hotels correctly, not by looking at raw prices from different dates or seasons but by aligning the conditions first and then reading the coefficients.
econ_section_significance_pvalue: For each coefficient the regression reports a p‑value. This number shows how compatible the estimated effect is with the idea that the true effect is actually zero: if the p‑value is small, it is unlikely that such a strong effect would appear just by chance; if it is large, the data are also consistent with there being no real effect at all.
econ_section_significance_threshold: Using the usual 5% rule, we call an effect statistically significant if p < 0.05 and statistically uncertain otherwise. If the p‑value is below 0.05, the data contain a clear signal that the coefficient is different from zero; if the p‑value is higher, the signal is weak and we should treat the estimated effect with caution.
econ_section_significance_difference: When reading the table it helps to separate two questions: how reliable is the number statistically, and how big is it in economic terms?
econ_section_significance_statistical: Statistical significance answers the first question. It tells us whether the coefficient is clearly different from zero given the noise in the data; a lower p‑value means a stronger signal, a higher p‑value means more uncertainty.
econ_section_significance_economic: Economic significance answers the second question. It asks whether the size of the effect, measured in USD, is big enough to matter. A coefficient of +0.5 USD may be statistically very precise but practically irrelevant, while a coefficient of +30 USD with wide confidence intervals can still be important for hotel managers thinking about their pricing.
econ_section_significance_insignificant: Some coefficients come out statistically insignificant for technical reasons rather than economic ones:
econ_section_significance_low_variation: Low variation: if a variable barely changes in the data (for example, almost all observations are in the same category), the model cannot learn its effect precisely and the p‑value will be high.
econ_section_significance_limited_sample: Limited sample: with few observations, standard errors are large, so even sizeable effects may not pass the 5% threshold and the p‑values remain high.
econ_section_significance_collinearity: Collinearity: if two variables move together too closely (for example, specific months and seasons), the model struggles to distinguish their separate effects. This inflates standard errors and makes otherwise meaningful effects look statistically weak.
econ_section_winter_title: Why Winter Is the Most Reliable Baseline
econ_section_winter_emphasis: Winter is used as the anchor season because it gives the cleanest and most stable picture of the market. In winter the regression has an easier job separating genuine price differences from one‑off events and tourism shocks.
econ_section_winter_stability: Winter demand stability: winter brings fewer extreme peaks in tourist arrivals, so prices move more smoothly. This makes the winter intercept and the winter‑based coefficients less sensitive to random spikes in demand.
econ_section_winter_tourism: Lower tourism shocks: unlike the high season, winter is less affected by festivals, major events or sudden tour flows. Prices in winter therefore tell us more about the underlying market conditions and less about short‑term scarcity.
econ_section_winter_predictable: More predictable pricing: when demand is calmer, hotels adjust prices less aggressively day by day. As a result, the relationships between season, city, hotel and price that the model estimates around the winter baseline are more stable and easier to interpret.
econ_section_winter_strategic: Less short‑term strategic pricing: in busy months hotels may change prices several times in response to bookings or events. In winter such tactical moves are rarer, so the regression captures more of the structural pricing pattern and less of the minute‑to‑minute tactics.
econ_section_winter_conclusion: Because of this, winter is a convenient reference point for the whole model: coefficients expressed relative to winter are typically more stable, and conclusions drawn from winter‑based comparisons are more robust than those taken from short peak periods.
econ_section_anova_what: Conceptually ANOVA asks the same question as a simple seasonal comparison chart: are the seasonal averages far enough apart that we can say the seasons really differ, or are the gaps small enough to be explained by noise? It does this with two numbers, the F‑statistic and the p‑value.
econ_section_anova_why: ANOVA is helpful next to the regression because it ignores everything except season and looks only at raw seasonal averages. If both ANOVA and the regression tell us that seasons matter, we have a consistent message coming from a simple tool and from a richer model that controls for city, hotel and time.
econ_section_anova_fstat: The F‑statistic is the summary of how much variation comes from differences between seasons compared with variation within seasons. This number shows how strong the seasonal pattern is overall: if it is higher, between‑season gaps dominate within‑season noise; if it is lower, most variation happens inside seasons and the seasonal pattern is weak.
econ_section_anova_fstat_strong: strong evidence that seasonal averages differ meaningfully
econ_section_anova_fstat_moderate: some evidence of seasonal differences, but not extremely strong
econ_section_anova_fstat_weak: only weak evidence that seasonal averages differ
econ_section_anova_pvalue: The ANOVA p‑value plays the same role as in regression. This number shows how likely it is to see seasonal gaps of this size if, in truth, all seasons had the same mean price: if it is small (below 0.05), we treat the seasonal differences as real; if it is larger, we say that the data do not clearly separate the seasons from each other.
econ_section_anova_pvalue_significant: that seasonal effects are statistically significant
econ_section_anova_pvalue_nonsignificant: that seasonal effects are not statistically significant at conventional levels
econ_section_questions_regression: Why regression instead of averages? Averages tell us where prices tend to be higher or lower, but they mix all effects together. Regression keeps the logic “all else equal”: it lets us say how much of the price comes from the season, how much from the city, how much from the hotel and how much from the time trend, with everything else held fixed.
econ_section_questions_winter: Why Winter as baseline? Winter gives the calmest market, with fewer tourism shocks and special events. Using it as the reference makes all seasonal coefficients easy to read (“how far from winter is this season?”) and makes the baseline less noisy.
econ_section_questions_fixed_effect: What does a hotel fixed effect mean? It is the average mark‑up or discount in USD that a hotel applies relative to the reference hotel once we hold season, city and time constant. In other words, it is the hotel’s own contribution to the price, coming from its quality, reputation, location and amenities.
econ_section_questions_causal: Is this model causal? Strictly speaking it is not: it is an OLS regression with good controls and hotel fixed effects, not a fully identified causal design. It greatly reduces many obvious biases by controlling for season, city, hotel and time, but a cautious reader should still treat the coefficients as well‑structured correlations rather than definitive causal effects.
econ_section_questions_limitations: What are the main limitations? (1) The relationships are assumed to be linear, so very sharp price jumps or non‑linear capacity effects are only approximated. (2) Time‑varying shocks that are not in the data (for example, marketing campaigns or sudden travel restrictions) are not modelled explicitly. (3) The model does not include strategic interactions between hotels. (4) Some potential endogeneity remains if hotels adjust prices in anticipation of demand shocks that we do not observe. Even with these caveats, the model gives a disciplined and easy‑to‑explain picture of the main price drivers.
